XXIX

On the following day Dounika, bareheaded and barefooted, came running to Sanine who was gardening.

тАЬVladimir Petrovitch,тАЭ she exclaimed, and her silly face had a scared look, тАЬthe officers have come, and they wish to speak to you.тАЭ She repeated the words like a lesson that she had learnt by heart.

Sanine was not surprised. He had been expecting a challenge from Sarudine.

тАЬAre they very anxious to see me?тАЭ he asked in a jocular tone.

Dounika, however, must have had an inkling of something dreadful, for instead of hiding her face she gazed at Sanine in sympathetic bewilderment.

Sanine propped his spade against a tree, tightened his belt and walked towards the house with his usual jaunty step.

тАЬWhat fools they are! What absolute idiots!тАЭ he said to himself, as he thought of Sarudine and his seconds. By this no insult was intended; it was just the sincere expression of his own opinion.

Passing through the house, he saw Lida coming out of her room. She stood on the threshold; her face white as a shroud, and her eyes, anxious and distressful. Her lips moved, yet no sound escaped from them. At that moment she felt that she was the guiltiest, most miserable woman in all the world.

In an armchair in the morning-room sat Maria Ivanovna, looking utterly helpless and panic-stricken. Her cap that resembled a cockтАЩs comb was poised sideways on her head, and she gazed in terror at Sanine, unable to utter a word. He smiled at her and was inclined to stop for a moment, yet he preferred to proceed.

Tanaroff and Von Deitz were sitting in the drawing-room bolt upright, with their heads close together, as if in their white tunics and tight riding-breeches they felt extremely uncomfortable. As Sanine entered they both rose slowly and with some hesitation, apparently uncertain how to behave.

тАЬGood day, gentlemen,тАЭ said Sanine in a loud voice, as he held out his hand.

Von Deitz hesitated, but Tanaroff bowed in such an exaggerated way that for an instant Sanine caught sight of the closely cropped hair at the back of his neck.

тАЬHow can I be of service to you?тАЭ continued Sanine, who had noticed TanaroffтАЩs excessive politeness, and was surprised at the assurance with which he played his part in this absurd comedy.

Von Deitz drew himself up and sought to give an expression of hauteur to his horse-like countenance; unsuccessfully, however, owing to his confusion. Strange to say, it was Tanaroff, usually so stupid and shy, who addressed Sanine in firm, decisive fashion.

тАЬOur friend, Victor Sergejevitsch Sarudine has done us the honour of asking us to represent him in a certain matter which concerns you and himself.тАЭ The sentence was delivered with automatic precision.

тАЬOho!тАЭ said Sanine with comic gravity, as he opened his mouth wide.

тАЬYes, sir,тАЭ continued Tanaroff, frowning slightly. тАЬHe considers that your behaviour towards him was notтБатАФerтБатАФquiteтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬYes, yes, I understand,тАЭ interrupted Sanine, losing patience.

тАЬI very nearly kicked him out of the house, so that тАШnotтБатАФerтБатАФquiteтАЩ is hardly the right way of putting it.тАЭ

The speech was lost upon Tanaroff, who went on:

тАЬWell, sir, he insists on your taking back your words.тАЭ

тАЬYes, yes,тАЭ chimed in the lanky Von Deitz, who kept shifting the position of his feet, like a stork.

Sanine smiled.

тАЬTake them back? How can I do that? тАШAs uncaged bird is spoken word!тАЩтАКтАЭ

Too perplexed to reply, Tanaroff looked Sanine full in the face.

тАЬWhat evil eyes he has!тАЭ thought the latter.

тАЬThis is no joking matter,тАЭ began Tanaroff, looking flushed and angry. тАЬAre you prepared to retract your words, or are you not?тАЭ

Sanine at first was silent.

тАЬWhat an utter idiot!тАЭ he thought, as he took a chair and sat down.

тАЬPossibly I might be willing to retract my words in order to please and pacify Sarudine,тАЭ he began, speaking seriously, тАЬthe more so as I attach not the slightest importance to them. But, in the first place, Sarudine, being a fool, would not understand my motive, and, instead of holding his tongue, would brag about it. In the second place, I thoroughly dislike Sarudine, so that, under these circumstances, I donтАЩt see that there is any sense in my retractation.тАЭ

тАЬVery well, thenтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ hissed Tanaroff through his teeth.

Von Deitz stared in amazement, and his long face turned yellow.

тАЬIn that caseтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ began Tanaroff, in a louder and would-be threatening tone.

Sanine felt fresh hatred for the fellow as he looked at his narrow forehead and his tight breeches.

тАЬYes, yes, I know all about it,тАЭ he interrupted. тАЬBut one thing, let me tell you; I donтАЩt intend to fight Sarudine.тАЭ

Von Deitz turned round sharply.

Tanaroff drew himself up, and said in a tone of contempt.

тАЬWhy not, pray?тАЭ

Sanine burst out laughing. His hatred had vanished as swiftly as it had come.

тАЬWell, this is why. First of all, I have no wish to kill Sarudine, and secondly, I have even less desire to be killed myself.тАЭ

тАЬButтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ began Tanaroff scornfully.

тАЬI wonтАЩt, and thereтАЩs an end of it!тАЭ said Sanine, as he rose. тАЬWhy, indeed? I donтАЩt feel inclined to give you any explanation. That were too much to expect, really!тАЭ

TanaroffтАЩs profound contempt for the man who refused to fight a duel was blended with the implicit belief that only an officer could possibly possess the pluck and the fine sense of honour necessary to do such a thing. That is why SanineтАЩs refusal did not surprise him in the least; in fact, he was secretly pleased.

тАЬThat is your affair,тАЭ he said, in an unmistakably contemptuous tone, тАЬbut I must warn you thatтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Sanine laughed.

тАЬYes, yes, I know, but I advise Sarudine not toтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬNot toтБатАФwhat?тАЭ asked Tanaroff, as he picked up his cap from the windowsill.

тАЬI advise him not to touch me, or else IтАЩll give him such a thrashing thatтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬLook here!тАЭ cried Von Deitz, in a fury. тАЬIтАЩm not going to stand thisтБатАКтБатАж YouтБатАКтБатАж you are simply laughing at us. DonтАЩt you understand that to refuse to accept a challenge isтБатАКтБатАж isтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

He was as red as a lobster, his eyes were starting from his head, and there was foam on his lips.

Sanine looked curiously at his mouth, and said:

тАЬAnd this is the man whose calls himself a disciple of Tolstoy!тАЭ

Von Deitz winced, and tossed his head.

тАЬI must beg of you,тАЭ he spluttered, ashamed all the while at thus addressing a man with whom till now he had been on friendly terms. тАЬI must beg of you not to mention that. It has nothing whatever to do with this matter.тАЭ

тАЬHasnтАЩt it! though?тАЭ replied Sanine. тАЬIt has a great deal to do with it.тАЭ

тАЬYes, but I must ask you,тАЭ croaked Von Deitz, becoming hysterical.

тАЬReally, this is too much! In shortтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOh! ThatтАЩll do!тАЭ replied Sanine, drawing back in disgust from Von Deitz, from whose mouth saliva spurted. тАЬThink what you like; I donтАЩt care. And tell Sarudine that he is an ass!тАЭ

тАЬYouтАЩve no right, sir, I say, youтАЩve no right,тАЭ shouted Von Deitz.

тАЬVery good, very good,тАЭ said Tanaroff, quite satisfied.

тАЬLet us go.тАЭ

тАЬNo!тАЭ cried the other, plaintively, as he waved his lanky arms. тАЬHow dare he?тБатАКтБатАж what business!тБатАКтБатАж ItтАЩs simplyтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Sanine looked at him, and, making a contemptuous gesture, walked out of the room.

тАЬWe will deliver your message to our brother-officer,тАЭ said Tanaroff, calling after him.

тАЬAs you please,тАЭ said Sanine, without looking round. He could hear Tanaroff trying to pacify the enraged Von Deitz, and thought to himself, тАЬAs a rule the fellowтАЩs an utter fool, but put him on his hobbyhorse, and he becomes quite sensible.тАЭ

тАЬThe matter cannot be allowed to rest thus!тАЭ cried the implacable Von Deitz, as they went out.

From the door of her room, Lida gently called тАЬVolodja!тАЭ

Sanine stood still.

тАЬWhat is it?тАЭ

тАЬCome here; I want to speak to you.тАЭ

Sanine entered LidaтАЩs little room where, owing to the trees in front of the window, soft green twilight reigned. There was a feminine odour of perfume and powder.

тАЬHow nice it is in here,тАЭ said Sanine, with a sigh of relief.

Lida stood facing the window, and green reflected lights from the garden flickered round her cheeks and shoulders.

тАЬWhat do you want with me?тАЭ he asked kindly.

Lida was silent, and she breathed heavily.

тАЬWhy, what is the matter?тАЭ

тАЬAre youтБатАФnot going to fight a duel?тАЭ she asked hoarsely, without looking round.

тАЬNo.тАЭ

Lida was silent.

тАЬWell, what of that?тАЭ said Sanine.

LidaтАЩs chin trembled. She turned sharply round and murmured quickly:

тАЬI canтАЩt understand that, I canтАЩtтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOh!тАЭ exclaimed Sanine, frowning. тАЬWell, IтАЩm very sorry for you.тАЭ

Human stupidity and malice surrounded him on all sides. To find such qualities alike in bad folk and good folk, in handsome people as in ugly, proved utterly disheartening.

He turned on his heels and went out.

Lida watched him go, and then, holding her head with both hands, she flung herself upon the bed. The long black plait lay at full length along the white coverlet. At this moment Lida, strong, supple and beautiful in spite of her despair, looked younger, more full of life than ever. Through the window came warmth and radiance from the garden, and the room was bright and pleasant. Yet of all this Lida saw nothing.
